title: 2025/12/03 - EW (Part 3)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Education and Workforce Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Education [Part 3]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Education
description: Wednesday 3 December 2025 | Education and Workforce Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Education Hearing of evidence: Ministry of Education Ellen MacGregor-Reid, Te Tumu Whakarae mō te Mātauranga | Secretary for Education Pauline Cleaver, Hautū, Te Poutāhū | Deputy Secretary - Curriculum Centre Kevin Martin, Chief Financial Officer Rob Campbell, Hautū, Te Pou Rangatōpū | Deputy Secretary – Corporate Andy Jackson, Hautū, Te Pou Kaupapahere | Deputy Secretary – Policy Mere-Hēni Simcock-Rēweti, Hautū, Te Pou Tuarongo | Deputy Secretary - Te Pou Tuarongo Helen Hurst, Acting Hautū, Te Pae Aronui | Acting Deputy Secretary – Operations and Integration Anna Welanyk, Hautū, Te Pou Ohumahi Mātauranga | Deputy Secretary - Education Workforce
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Education. Ministry of Education. Ellen MacGregor-Reid, Te Tumu Whakarae mō te Mātauranga

chunks.text: Uh, well, good afternoon, Ellen Or, and good afternoon to your team. And welcome back to Education and Workforce. Thank you for, um, scheduling time in to spend with us this afternoon. Um, as you know, uh, we follow a structured agenda, um, in this committee. And there, w- we will try and stick broadly to the, or we will stick broadly to the headings as we go through the afternoon.

Um, but, uh, allow some free flow with the sub-headings. Kick off with some introductory remarks from yourself, um, and then I'll open up to questions from the committee. Well, kia ora koutou. Uh, I'm Ellen MacGregory, Secretary for Education. I appreciate you making time for me to come, uh, and spend with you this afternoon. I have some members of my leadership team here with me today. Um, I may invite them to the table from time to time, um, and I'll make sure that they introduce themselves, uh, and where they're

from. Uh, recognize that, uh, we only get this opportunity once a year, and time is valuable, so, um, I won't make any further opening remarks, and I'm happy to move into questions. Great. Thank you, Ellen. Lawrence. Um, thank you all for coming, and first of all, congratulations, Ellen.

Mm-hmm. Um, we're gonna start in terms of the structured agenda. The first part is early learning. And I wanna start with something that, um, we just heard from ERO. 'Cause s- so just before we had ERO, in one of the reports that they've done, they look at the fact that 47%, um, in, of the ECE sector, and I think the term they used was, um, independent or et cetera, I can't remember the exact term...

Oh, standalone services, 47% fell below the expectation for quality learning. Mm. And I wondered, to start off with, what is then MOE doing to take onboard the feedback? Mm. And how does that feature into what you are seeing in terms of some of the ECE reforms, um, and how that would enhance, uh, quality of teaching and learning within the ECE space?

Mm. So generally across, um, ERO recommendations, my posture is unless we have a really good reason not to act on them, we act on them. Um, with ECE, um, we have a, uh, range of staff who work directly with ECE around their licensing, which includes quality of ECE, and have professional development available, um, for that quality curriculum. Um, we do have a range of curriculum tools available. I might ask Pauline Cleaver, Deputy Secretary for the Curriculum please to come to the table, and just talk quickly about Kōwhiri

Whakapae. Um, Kōwhiri Whakapae are a range of tools that support, um, teachers implement, uh, the Te Whariki, the early learning curriculum. These practice tools support teachers, uh, in areas of oral language, social and emotional learning, and mathematics, as being key areas of, um, you know, uh, key concepts for learning, uh, that supports go- uh, young people in, in their journey towards schooling.

Um, it helps them with their early literacy, uh, that, uh, transition into school, being able to relate with others, uh, and manage themselves, uh, as they, uh, start their formal education journey. We've got a range of tools and resources that support that, and professional learning and development available for teachers online, and through a range of, uh, videos, et cetera, uh, that teachers can access in their own time, uh, to develop their skills, their practices- Mm.

... uh, in those areas. And those tools have been very well received. Um, but we are also looking at regulatory reform. Andy. Yes. So, um, you'll be aware that a, a substantial, uh, review of the regulatory system for early childhood education is now largely been concluded. Um, we've recently, uh, gazetted new licensing criteria that follow on from that review.

Question from Adam. And, um, obviously there's a, um, a significant shift, uh, to come in the form of the transfer of the Ministry's regulatory functions to the Education Review Office, pending, um, you know, the legislative program that's, um, currently before the House. And so, um, those changes taken together are designed to increase the quality of ECE regulation, to provide services with a clearer understanding of the criteria that they need to meet, and, um, a, a stronger, um, relationship with the regulator that has a range of graduated interventions

available for it to use. And so that's a role that ERO will be taking on. Yeah. Um, so just supplementary questions. Uh, I've got a couple, um, Chair. (clears throat) The first of, the, one of the things you mentioned in terms of Te Whariki and the tools that's available, that's great. But what we're seeing is- Mm-hmm.... that not all ECE centers, and particularly, you know, there's disproportionate amount of qualified teachers within that. So I'm guessing that for qualified teachers, I have no issues, I'm assured that there's access to that.

Mm-hmm. But what about the non-qualified, um, staff in ECE centers? Would they also have access to... Are they also-... expected to meet that? Um, both, they both, they both have- Okay.... um, they both have access to. And irrespective of the type of service, um, we, we are expecting that, um, they are using Te Whariki as the basis of their program. And that the tools support the, uh, teachers, qualified or unqualified, um, to practice, um, they, they build their practice and, and provide the best programs for our young

people. Um, just two final stops, Chair. One of them is around the licensing criteria that you just mentioned. One of the things that I did notice, I'm glad to see that the sleep checks are still back in. But one of the things that is no longer al- um, in there is that services no longer need a philosophy statement. And I just wanna check with you on some of the rationales behind some of the value-based statements that you, we normally expect to see in licensing criteria.

So, um, the licensing criteria have a range of purposes. Obviously, the primary purpose of most of the criteria is the health, safety, and wellbeing of children, and that, um, is a substantial proportion of the 98 or so criteria that, that predated the regulatory review. Um, there are a number of criteria that relate to the curriculum and its implementation, and obviously that connects to the conversation that, that you were just having with Pauline.

Um, and then there are a small number of other criteria that relate to things like the, the purpose and nature of the organization and its governance. One of the things that the regulatory review found was that requiring a center to have a philosophy statement was not the sort of thing that was appropriate for a regulatory policy instrument.

Okay. So obviously, if a center wants to appeal to a particular parent community through a statement about what its purpose is, that's absolutely appropriate for it, and you'll have, um, uh, you know, networks like Montessori, for example, which have very clear educational philosophy. But, um, for most ECE services, requiring by law that they publish a philosophy statement is not something that the Ministry for Regulation found was contributing to the wellbeing, safety, or educational outcomes for children, so...

Final question, because you mentioned regulation. There's gonna be the new Director of Regulation. Mm-hmm. One of the things we couldn't get clarity on was how that interact with you. I know that as secretary, you're the ones who appoint that. Mm-hmm. But the clarification we're trying to get is the organizational chart of how that is going to sit. So the example that we used previously is in the current situation, and we'll talk about later- Mm-hmm.

... that the person who is fronting on all that was the Deputy Secretary of Operations. But then if now some of that sits under Director of Regulations- Mm-hmm.... so how does that- Mm-hmm. ... directorship sits across with the deputy secretary level, Andy, like yourself and Pauline? Sure. The important thing with the Director of Regulation is it's someone who holds that office. They can have a-another substantive job, but they hold that office and are clear about their role as the regulator.

Mm-hmm. So I will be appointing someone to be the Director of Regulation should the legislation pass. Yeah. Uh, and that will sit within our current Operations and Integration shop, noting, uh, that I have announced a change to the Ministry's leadership structure, which comes into play at the beginning of March next year, where I'm bringing the operational arms closer together.

Okay, thank you very much. Um, Parmjeet, your side. Thank you. So with the update of, uh, the ECE licensing criteria, uh, that is going to come- (coughs)... into effect next year, um, just want to know how you're gearing up for that, and what does that involve, um, at your end and how you are ensuring that everybody, the sector is kind of ready for that?

Mm-hmm. So at the moment, we're busy creating, um, guidance, guidelines, templates, a range of materials that both my staff can use but also can be provided out to the sector. We are doing that in concert with ERO, recognizing that there is currently, uh, a bill, which I think is before this committee- Mm-hmm.

... uh, which is considering transferring those functions through to ERO. So we're getting ready for, um, the graduated, uh, interventions and for the licensing criteria with training, with guidance, with guidelines, with complaints. Good. Okay. Uh, Jan. Thank you. Thank you, and I'll offer my congratulations too, Ellen.

That's wonderful news. Um, in the early learning space, and I'm only here for 20 minutes, so I'm going to take the opportunity to ask about financials in early learning. And on page 171 of the annual report, it talks about early learning education as being $211 million underspent, mainly due to $173 million of funding approved in the budget to manage risk associated with demand-driven forecasting of early learning subsidies being not required.

That's a heck of a big, um, difference there, $173 million. Yeah. Why do you think that has happened, that we don't have those, um, the underspend and the funded child hours there? Uh, so our demand-driven appropriation for early learning is based on forecasts around s- um, child numbers and child hours. Um, in the last financial year, the '23/'24, we've reached that appropriation because we under forecast the number of places needed, the number of hours needed.

It is quite an art to forecast, uh, early learning, uh, volume, um, the volume and places. It sort of depends on a range of factors, immigration, job markets, parental decisions. So for the more recent, uh, budget round, we're a little bit more conservative in our forecasts to avoid breaching our appropriations. Uh, but it doesn't reflect, uh, an underspend in terms of children not receiving service they should or families not receiving subsidies they should.

I guess I'm more interested in that respect around the fact, do, do you do the work, um, and I'm assuming you do, but I want to sort of- Mm-hmm.... sort of get into it a bit more granular around where are those children not turning up? What types of services? Do you do that sort of work around the, the, um, demographics that we're not seeing turning up?

Like, I, I get that it's a very hard job to do the forecasting- (laughs) But-... what, what sort of demographics are using the services more than others? Sure. Um, in terms of our forecast, we use overall population figures. Um, and happy to provide the committee with details of how we do our forecasting.

Uh, we do track, uh, ECE attendance and uptake, and we can provide the committee with a breakdown of, of where we see different, uh, population-... groups in terms of their use of ECE, uh, and where there are, there are areas of higher demand and lower demand.

Um, one Do you want that, do you want that- Yes, I do. That would be very helpful.... recorded? Thank you. Yeah. Yes, very helpful. Um, one more question. So, there was 173 million dollars, which you've explained in the annual report- Mm-hmm.... but that means there's still 38 million dollars underspend. Where does that, what's covered by that 38 million dollars?

Mm-hmm. Sorry, which, uh- Uh, page 171. Of the annual report? Um, it's 171 on the online version. I haven't got- Oh, okay.... a hard copy in front of me. All right. Um, sorry, I'll just try and find it in the hard copy. Yeah. If I had the hard copy in front of me, which I don't.

(clears throat) Yeah. Yes, my apologies. I'm in 171 in my copy. I'm on, I'm on, um, adult education. Yeah, no. Thank you. They do, they do do a bit different. (laughs) Yes, they do. Um, I can't actually find the- Yeah, maybe w- we'll give-... statement you're on. ... we'll, we'll put that as either a come back to, or if you find it, in the meantime, we'll- Oh, it's 174.

... in the hard copy. 174 in the non-departmental, departmental output expenses. Early learning education was 211, seven% under budget. 7% under budget due to 173 of funding approved to manage the demand-driven appropriation. So, that still leaves 38 million- Underspend.

... underspent. I was just wondering what that was. So, I'll anticipate if I have resulting underspend of funded child hours, I'll have to come back to you on the residual- Okay. That would be super helpful. Thank you. We'll put that as a, um, follow-up question. Yeah. Thanks, Mike. Um, Chana. (speaks Te Reo Māori) Um, thank you so much, uh, for, um, being here today.

Um, my questions in (speaks Te Reo Māori) are just around early childhood education ref- reform. Uh, the annual report referred to ECE reform and the implementation of recommendations from the government's review of ECE regulation oversight, and a new director of regulations.

My (speaks Te Reo Māori) is just around how has the WAI claim 2336 Matua Rautia report on the Kohanga Reo claim informed implementations of recommendations, if at all. Uh, the ECE regulations... Andy, you'll correct me if I'm wrong. I, I don't think, uh, impacting through into Kohanga Reo in the same way.

Uh, they, uh... So that's, uh, they're excluded from the funding review but- Yes. Okay. ... they are subject to the, the regulatory changes. Okay. Yeah. Um, yeah. So, I mean, we work very closely with Kohanga Reo. Um, the result of that, uh, tribunal claim has been that we've had a very strong, um, partnership relationship with them- Yeah.... uh, that's worked over successive governments, um, in terms of things like changes to their funding model- Mm-hmm.

... um, and changes to the way in which we fund property for Kohanga Reo. Um, we've, uh, the, both the Ministry for Regulation and ourselves kept very close to Kohanga through that relationship during the regulatory review and, and subsequent to it. Uh, and we've ensured that they were v- you know, fully across any of the changes that have been taking place and were fully consulted through that process. I'm not aware of any particular concerns that Kohanga have- Okay.

Fine. ... expressed to us in relation to the regulatory review. Um, and, um, obviously if that's, if that... I'm not across a- e- all of the consultation responses- Mm-hmm.... um, but I'm not aware of any particularly significant concerns that they've expressed. Cool. Cool. Um, just supplementaring off that. Um, how will the Ministry deal with the regulatory challenges faced by Kohanga, both as a part of the government's recent review of ECE, and as a part of the Kohanga Reo's independent trey claim?

Um, so I think the m- main, uh, way in which we'll do that is by seeking to, uh, preserve and enhance the quality of our ongoing relationship with them. Um, I, I, I think, I think that will mean we can both keep across changes as they're rolled out and we'll be certainly wanting to understand their experience of the changes in the regulatory review.

So- Cool.... yeah. Thank you. Um, while we're talking about regulatory review in early childhood, since the September 2022 report, uh, by ERO on recommendations to enhance initial, um, teacher education on teaching disabled children in early, uh, learning settings, what work, um, has the Ministry undertaken to address that?

We've undertaken a range of work, both around initial teacher education, um, in terms of understanding the quality of that ITE, how well it's preparing teachers, both the early learning and schooling to, to enter the setting or the service. Um, we continually work with a range of, um, disability sector, um, representatives and providers to understand, uh, what more needs to be done and should be done, and provide guidance through to, through to teachers and through to services.

Ha- have you had any, um, update from ERO on that report and that implementation that you're aware of, or any quality check internally that you've done to give a sense of whether that has worked and addressed those recommendations? I'm not aware of an ERO follow-up yet. Uh, but generally, we have a process in place where ERO will follow up after a period of time.

Okay. Great. Uh, Lawrence. Thank you. Um, just actually picking up from what the chair has said, um, this might not be something that, uh, mm, MOE funds, but can you just first of all clarify who funds the, um, education support worker hours?...

for ECE infants, students under four and a half. No, the early intervention teachers? Not... The early intervention teachers, yeah. Mm-hmm. And, yep, so that's something that you fund. And as well as the ESW hours? Yeah. Yes. Yes. Yeah. Can I just check, so recently, it's been reported on that the, there's been a tightening of some of the criteria and now s- for students, for kids in ECE- Yes.

... they're now getting up to five hours a week, and how that five hours is being used- Mm-hmm.... is very much ad hoc. Sometimes the one hour that they get a day is during nap time, where the students may not need it. Can I just check, in terms of whether there's been any sort of, uh, funding change or how that funding has been- Mm-hmm.

... been calculated? I'm, I'm not aware of a funding change, but Helen's coming to the table- (laughs) Um, and she may be aware of the change. Kia ora. Helen Hurst, um, I'm the acting deputy secretary for operations and integration at the Ministry. Yeah. Um, so there's a range of different, um, support worker allowances that, um, are very much based on need. So, we haven't made any funding changes.

Um, we continually look at, at, um, the resource that we have available and match that to need, but there is not a, a funding change. Can I just check, what is the maximum number of hours, um, a s- a child can get for ESW? It, it will depend on their need, so, uh, uh, and, and how many hours they attend.

So, there is no maximum? So, they could get 20 hours. Potentially. If that's what they need. Absolutely. Interesting. Yeah, yeah. 'Cause I don't think that has been communicated because I think there, when people have applied they have been told that there is a maximum and there is a cap.

But what you're saying that there isn't one? The- there will be a cap in terms of the r- the r- staffing available. So, there may need to be some- Ah.... decisions made practically around that in different locations. But, um, generally the, the assessment is based on the need of the child. Okay. One final question on this, which is if the s- the staffing capacity is an issue, have you actually heard if, if s- kids are getting fewer hours because there's not enough staff?

It's not something I'm aware of. Okay. Cool. Okay. Uh, that's a nice end to part one. And we're gonna move to, uh, part two. We're moving. Primary and secondary, um, schooling. We've got an hour for this section. Um, Lauren (laughs) 'cause... Ooh. Question, yeah.

Excellent. You have to be, you have to get in there. (laughs) Um, we're gonna start with in terms of some of the, the reasons and probably the most topical issue right now- Mm-hmm.... which is, what was ME's, MOE's role and consultation process around the removal of Te Tiriti o Waitangi from section 127 of the Education and Training Act?

Uh, so, um, w- our advice has been proactively released, um, if you would like to- What did it say? So, what it said was that, um, we thought the government should consult, um, before making changes to legislation. Um, but that is ultimately a decision for cabinets to make. Um, we noted that changing section 127, um, uh, would likely be controversial and at a time when there is a lot of important work occurring.

Um, ultimately, as you'll see from the proactive releases, um, ministers also had advice from, uh, the Treaty References Review Group and cabinets get advice from a range of sources, which noted that, um, cabinet should consider or may want to consider whether it's appropriate for boards of trustees, given that they are entities a number of steps removed from the Crown, to have the obligation of the Crown to give effect to Te Tiriti. However, the decision that was made was that section 127 still needs to have boards provide

equitable outcomes for Maori students, uh, which is fundamental. Uh, that they need to take steps for te reo Maori to be provided. And they need to take reasonable steps to reflect cultural diversity in Te Ao Maori. So, important elements, uh, that MOE advised should be retained were retained.

Okay. So, uh, we'll take a couple of sups, but if it's, if we're gonna focus on stuff that's already in the public domain, then there are other things that we need to focus on, in terms of the review, Well, I mean, all of it's in the public domain. Anything we're referring to- Well, so let's ask- Yeah.... questions that are either gonna be additive- Yeah.... um, or we'll move on. No, sure. Lauren? Um, fair point, Chair. Thank you. I guess, um, the, the follow-up question around that is thank you for highlighting some of that.

Um, is then one of the other things that's removed that was also not just simply Te Tiriti o Waitangi and you mentioned in terms of equitable outcomes. But one of the other things that has been removed from that is taking into consideration Tikanga Maori and, uh, local mataranga Maori- Mm-hmm.... from that as a whole package. Um, I was wondering if that is also something that you have given advice on, not simply just the Te Tiriti part, but also the other part? And I guess a second part to that question is, um, do you know of any jurisdictions

overseas where an official language of a country is optional for people to learn, sure, but it's written in a way that is only available if someone asks? Does that make sense in terms of the law? Yes, it does. Okay. So, the first part of your question, sorry, I lost the first part. Was around the, the, the removal of Tikanga Maori and local mataranga Maori from that whole package as well, not just simply the Te Tiriti- Mm-hmm. Did you want to say something Andrew?

Oh, uh, yes, I mean, our advice also recommended- Okay.... the retention of reference in particular to Tikanga Maori- Yeah.... uh, in schools. Um, and so that's-... the answer to the, the first part of your question, I think. I, I do think, um, though, in terms of, um, what I'm seeing is clearly the choice is still there for schools, and schools will still be using that choice, and in fact, we expect them to do so.

Right. And the second part? I'm gonna go to... Oh, sorry. Yeah, there was a second part regarding the international part. Oh, the second part, the international. Um- Yeah. In terms of official languages, that's only available u- upon request and not naturally available, but off- optional for people to take. I am not myself fully across all jurisdictions and their official languages. I don't know, Angie, if you are. We can come back to you on that.

That'd be great, yeah. Thank you. Yes. Yes. I think only come back if it's advice that, that you looked at. Otherwise, it's a great question for the library. Oh, yeah. Yes, yes. Okay. Absolutely. Let, let, let's leave... Don't worry about having to investigate. Okay. Yes. (laughs) Only if you, only if there was advice. Otherwise, that's a good question for the library. Yes.

Pamjit? Yeah. So, in regards to this, uh, question, just want to understand from you, when a school board, uh, adopts a political position without consulting with the parent body, what options, uh, in your view parents, um, have to hold them to account? (sighs) Well, ultimately, um, schools are part of the wider- Mm-hmm. ... uh, government. Uh, we, uh, do expect teachers to, um, not express political views within the classroom or, um, unduly influence children. Ultimately, boards of trustees

are, you know, elected members from local communities. Mm-hmm. Um, I think if parents aren't happy with the performance of their school board, um, that is something for parents to raise with their board. Uh, and the Ministry will become involved where relationships break down. Thanks, and just for- Can I... Just before your next up, Pamjit- Yep.

Just before your next, for everyone- Yep. ... if we're not asking about the Ministry's activity, I'm going to move us on. Like, 'cause otherwise we're just asking general questions that we can go and research in the library. So, let's get back to the annual review and questions- Yeah, so- ... in the period. Pamjit. Yeah. So then, what proportion of school boards have a majority of seats, uh, being reserved for parent elected members, because they're teacher elected and staff, principal, everybody.

So, I'm just thinking what kind of influence, as you were saying, the elections have. Uh, so you have a principal member, a staff member, a student member, and then a- Minimum three.... m- yeah, I was gonna say a minimum of three elected. So, half the board- So, do you know if there are any reserve, like maj- how many schools would have like majority of members as parents?

No. Those are parents. Yeah. Another good question for the library. I think most school boards would. Mm. Um, most school, schools will have majority parents? Mm... Okay. Thanks, Pamjit. Let me just get just- No, that's all right. ... the thinking. I wanna... I'm, I'm, I've got to be consistent across everyone. I just think it's very important they are trans- It's a great question for the library. Hannah.

No, Mr. Chair, all questions can be put to the library. No, all done. Mr. Chair? Not all questions can. No, Mr. Chair, point of order. Mm. They were still in the thinking process, and I think they were just about to finish the word saying most, and I was getting that confirmation when you interrupted. Pamjit. I just want- I've been consistent with everyone.

I'll let, I'll let Alan answer that, and then we're moving on. I, I think most do. Mm. But we second that. Mm. Okay. If it is what she said, Pamjit. Yes. Yes. Right. I think we're... Look, we're going to move on from this topic, so I'll move to the next, um, primary. Willow-Jean, if you've got a... Your, your next primary? On a new topic, primary.

Oh, sorry. (clears throat) Primary Lawrence, new topic. (laughs) Got to be quick. (laughs) Um, I want to talk about, I, I guess, learning support. Um, so I know that... So this particularly part two, I know we're going to spend a lot of time, and there are quite a few things to cover. So one of the things that we know that is, um, Kahui Akos being sort of removed, uh, and the last time we checked, RTM and RT Lit are- We're going to have them. ...

finishing at the end of this year. Um, in... There's been an announcement being made, and yeah, I see that, uh, LSCs are going to be implemented next year, starting from term one. That's right. Um, some LSCs are already there. Mm-hmm. But I wondered if MOE has provided any advice or has, um, any information around... Okay. So, LSCs, the announcement is every school will have access to LSC.

Mm-hmm. But then that is at the end of the three years. So, in... But what we do then see is a vacuum in which RTMs and RT Lits and Kahui Akos are no longer available, and LSCs are not going to be in every single schools. In terms of the preparation work done by MOE- Mm-hmm.... what advice have you been given, or what information do you have on schools managing a lack of LSC in this transitional period, but at the same time has already lost RTM and RT Lit?

Okay. And in terms of LSCs, um, more schools will have LSCs than currently have LSCs. So, we're not removing an LSC from a school. So just- That's not what... Yep, just- Yep. Yep. In terms of RTMs and RT Lits, for RT Lits, at the same time as those were removed, we have surged a massive amount of resource and support into literacy and into numeracy, including new FTTE for schools. Pauline can provide all the numbers on- That's helpful.

... oh, and if Pauline would like to come to the table on those FPTT... FTTE- Yeah.... around RT Lit. Uh, RTMs were for Te Reo Maori, not for learning support. Uh, and, uh, as was announced at the time, the coverage of the RTMs was mixed. Not everyone accessed them- Mm.

... and decisions were made in budget to reallocate the money, so there was a fairer spread of support for-... Maori. So, my expectation and my advice is schools, while some schools may have had an RT-Lit, um, that they had just to themselves, um, more schools now have access to literacy support, both at the universal level with all of the in-class teaching resources- Mm.

... teaching practices, and we have additional FTTE- Mm.... to support literacy. But Pauline, do you want to run through the numbers? Yep. So, um, before, uh, the disestablishment of the, uh, T-Lits, um, 970 schools were getting FTTE to support their literacy interventions, so that is providing that support for students who need additional learning support in literacy.

Mm-hmm. Um, we are currently, with the additional FTE, looking to provide that, that FTE to 1,241 schools, uh, in, from the start of next year. We've also, of course, uh, added 143 FTE- Mm-hmm.... for mathematics support, um, that will be allocated out into schools. We haven't quite finished that allocation yet, um, and I'd be able to confirm those numbers when we have.

That'd be helpful. Um, just supplementary on the sort of the Learning Access Package. Um, one of the things we saw is that every child that is eligible for ORS will be, will have that ORS funding, but we're still hearing that, um, there's still a bottleneck in terms of application and access into the ORS structure.

Can I just check if you are able to confirm that every child who have, able to get ORS funding have received ORS funding? We have been tracking the ORS applications and approval rates. Can, can confirm they have been tracking up. So, while the criteria for ORS have not changed- Yes.... we are making a lot of headway through ORS applications and more children now are receiving ORS funding.
